title: Taiwan defense budget gridlock continues as US pressure intensifies ahead of May 6 negotiations
summary: Taiwan's Legislative Yuan remains deadlocked over the special defense budget, with DPP proposing $40 billion, KMT offering $12 billion, and US officials urging quick passage including funding for the $11.1 billion HIMARS arms package.
contents:
## Taiwan Defense Budget Stalemate Persists Despite US Pressure

### Budget Deadlock
Taiwan's Legislative Yuan remains gridlocked over competing versions of a special defense budget to fund asymmetric capabilities. Negotiations between the DPP, KMT, and TPP caucuses on April 23 and April 27 ended without consensus on procurement items or total funding amounts. The next negotiation session convenes on May 6.

### Competing Proposals
The Cabinet and DPP propose approximately $40 billion including $20.2 billion for future US arms purchases and $9.5 billion for domestic arms manufacturing. The KMT and TPP bills allocate around $12 billion. An internal KMT faction led by legislator Hsu Chiao-hsin advocates for approximately $25 billion, creating intra-party tension.

### US Lobbying Campaign
US Indo-Pacific Commander Admiral Samuel Paparo and American Institute in Taiwan Director Raymond Greene both called for rapid passage, emphasizing the $11.1 billion arms package approved in December 2025. The deadlock has already delayed Taiwan's payment for High Mobility Artillery Rocket Systems (HIMARS).

### KMT-US Dynamics
KMT Chairwoman Cheng Li-wun plans a ten-day US visit in June, stating she aims to alter "Cold War thinking" and improve US views of the PRC. She declared passing the Cabinet's $40 billion budget "absolutely impossible" and insisted no funding for future US arms sales would proceed without formal letters of offer.

**Source:** ISW-AEI China & Taiwan Update
